title: AI Agent Engineering Systems
subtitle: Design, ship, and scale agents that actually work — from first tool call to production-grade autonomy
description: An agent-specific course built from primary engineering sources: OpenAI's Agents SDK, Anthropic's tool-use guidance, the Model Context Protocol spec, and lessons published by teams shipping agents at scale. Covers the agent loop, tool design, multi-agent patterns, memory, evals, safety, and the launch checklist that separates demo agents from production ones.
audience: Engineers building AI agents — chatbots that take actions, research agents, customer-service agents, internal automation agents. Comfortable with at least one model provider's API and basic distributed-systems concepts.
prerequisites: Built at least one LLM-powered feature before (chat completion, RAG, etc.)
Useful but not required: the OpenAI or Anthropic course in this catalog
outcomes: Explain what an agent is — and isn't — and when to reach for one
Design tools that agents can use reliably
Build the agent loop end-to-end with timeouts, approvals, and observability
Apply multi-agent patterns (routing, hand-off, supervisor) when single-agent isn't enough
Implement short and long-term memory without the common pitfalls
Run evals that catch agent regressions before users do
Ship agents through a production checklist with cost, safety, and reliability controls

modules.title: What an Agent Actually Is
modules.summary: Define agent vs. workflow, the agent loop, and the patterns that show up across every framework.

lessons.title: Agent vs. workflow — when each is the right tool
lessons.objective: Distinguish a pipeline from an agent and pick the right pattern for the task.

lessons.title: The universal agent loop
lessons.objective: Implement the act-observe loop that every agent framework reduces to.

lessons.title: Tools, environment, and the action space
lessons.objective: Define what an agent can do, see, and change.

lessons.title: When NOT to build an agent
lessons.objective: Recognize the cases where a simple workflow or single LLM call beats an agent.

modules.title: Designing Tools Agents Can Actually Use
modules.summary: Good tool design is what makes an agent reliable. Naming, schemas, descriptions, error handling, and approvals.

lessons.title: Writing tool descriptions models actually read
lessons.objective: Write tool descriptions that drive correct tool selection.

lessons.title: Schemas, validation, and structured arguments
lessons.objective: Constrain inputs with JSON schemas so agents can't misfire.

lessons.title: Idempotency, retries, and error returns
lessons.objective: Design tools that survive being called twice and fail gracefully.

lessons.title: Approval gates and human-in-the-loop
lessons.objective: Add review steps on irreversible tool calls without breaking the agent's flow.

modules.title: Building a Production Agent
modules.summary: The end-to-end loop with planning, observability, cost control, and graceful failure.

lessons.title: Plan-then-act vs. react — which loop fits your problem
lessons.objective: Pick a loop architecture and avoid the wrong-loop-for-the-task trap.

lessons.title: Observability — tracing, logging, debugging multi-step systems
lessons.objective: Make agent runs debuggable in production.

lessons.title: Step caps, timeouts, and circuit breakers
lessons.objective: Prevent runaway loops and runaway bills.

lessons.title: Streaming agents to the user — UX patterns
lessons.objective: Surface progress so a 30-second agent feels responsive.

modules.title: Multi-Agent and Memory Patterns
modules.summary: When one agent isn't enough — routing, supervisor, hand-off — and how to give agents memory that doesn't sabotage them.

lessons.title: Single-agent vs. multi-agent — when complexity is justified
lessons.objective: Decide whether routing, supervisor, or hand-off patterns fit.

lessons.title: Routing, supervisor, and hand-off patterns
lessons.objective: Implement the three production multi-agent topologies.

lessons.title: Short-term memory — context, summaries, and compaction
lessons.objective: Manage what the agent remembers within a session.

lessons.title: Long-term memory — vectors, episodic stores, and the lessons learned
lessons.objective: Build memory that survives sessions without poisoning future runs.

modules.title: Evals, Safety, and Shipping
modules.summary: What separates a demo agent from a production one — evals, safety, monitoring, the launch checklist.

lessons.title: Agent evals — testing multi-step behavior
lessons.objective: Build an eval that catches multi-step regressions, not just single-shot output.

lessons.title: Safety patterns specific to agents
lessons.objective: Apply the safety controls that matter most for agents — beyond single-call safety.

lessons.title: Cost monitoring and the long tail of agent runs
lessons.objective: Catch agent cost regressions before they become incidents.

lessons.title: The agent launch checklist — from prototype to live system
lessons.objective: Walk the full pre-launch checklist for any agent.
